items.title: Complete MCP inventory documented
items.description: Configs, transports, owners, and credential scope for every server.
items.how_to_find: Collect every MCP config (IDE, gateway, CI). Record transport, binary path, package version, owner, and which secrets each server can reach.
items.how_to_fix: Maintain a living register of MCP servers with owner, data classification, and credential scope. Review quarterly and on every config change.

items.title: No shell wrappers in STDIO command
items.description: Block sh -c and unpinned npx package installs in MCP config.
items.how_to_find: Audit `command` and `args` in MCP configs. Flag `sh -c`, bare `npx`, and unpinned package tags. Confirm only approved launchers can start servers.
items.how_to_fix: Run MCP servers via pinned binaries or container images - never `sh -c` or floating `npx` installs. Block unapproved launchers in IDE policy.

items.title: Config changes require review
items.description: MCP configs in git need code review and hash pinning or re-approval.
items.how_to_find: Review Inventory and Configuration for config changes require review. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Pinned package versions
items.description: Lock MCP server package versions; reject floating latest tags.
items.how_to_find: In Inventory and Configuration: In Inventory and Configuration: In Inventory and Configuration: In Inventory and Configuration: Lock MCP server package versions; reject floating latest tags. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Pin package versions and image digests in config. Fail CI when configs reference `latest` or unpinned npm tags.

items.title: Separate OS user or container per server
items.description: MCP processes must not share host secrets namespace with agents.
items.how_to_find: Review Inventory and Configuration for separate os user or container per server. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Tool descriptions reviewed
items.description: Hunt hidden instructions, abnormal length, and adversarial schema text.
items.how_to_find: Pull raw tool schemas via MCP Inspector. Look for hidden markdown, base64 blobs, or instruction-like text in descriptions and parameter docs.
items.how_to_fix: Review third-party tool schemas before enablement. Strip marketing prose from descriptions and reject schemas with instruction-like hidden text.

items.title: Remote MCP requires authentication
items.description: tools/list and tools/call must not be anonymously reachable.
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.

items.title: Cross-server shadowing tested
items.description: Multi-server sessions checked for one server hijacking another tool.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Trust and Transport: In Tool Trust and Transport: In Tool Trust and Transport: In Tool Trust and Transport: Multi-server sessions checked for one server hijacking another tool. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Human approval for destructive tools
items.description: File delete, email send, SQL, payments require explicit confirm.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Trust and Transport: In Tool Trust and Transport: In Tool Trust and Transport: In Tool Trust and Transport: File delete, email send, SQL, payments require explicit confirm. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Gate delete, pay, email, and production-write tools behind explicit human approval with context shown to the reviewer.

items.title: HTTP MCP not exposed to internet
items.description: No unauthenticated MCP endpoints on public interfaces.
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.

items.title: Known CVE versions patched
items.description: LiteLLM, Cursor, MCP Inspector, gateway stack at patched versions.
items.how_to_find: Review Patching and Monitoring for known cve versions patched. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Pin package versions and image digests in config. Fail CI when configs reference `latest` or unpinned npm tags.

items.title: Logging on new server registration
items.description: Alert when new MCP servers or unexpected child processes spawn.
items.how_to_find: Review Patching and Monitoring for logging on new server registration. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: User content isolation from privileged tools
items.description: Untrusted prompts cannot reach high-privilege MCP without approval.
items.how_to_find: Review Patching and Monitoring for user content isolation from privileged tools. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Credential scoping per server
items.description: Fine-grained PATs per repo, not org-wide admin tokens.
items.how_to_find: Review Patching and Monitoring for credential scoping per server. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Pin package versions and image digests in config. Fail CI when configs reference `latest` or unpinned npm tags.

items.title: Registry provenance verified
items.description: Only install MCP packages from signed releases or org-owned repos.
items.how_to_find: Review Patching and Monitoring for registry provenance verified. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Hidden instructions in tool descriptions
items.description: Tool descriptions and parameter docs are injected directly into the model context.
items.how_to_find: Pull raw tool schemas via MCP Inspector. Look for hidden markdown, base64 blobs, or instruction-like text in descriptions and parameter docs.
items.how_to_fix: Review third-party tool schemas before enablement. Strip marketing prose from descriptions and reject schemas with instruction-like hidden text.

items.title: Invisible / homoglyph payloads in metadata
items.description: Zero-width characters, Unicode tag blocks, and homoglyphs can hide instructions inside otherwise benign-looking tool metadata, defeating human review while still reaching the model.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: Verify the client strips or flags non-printable characters before display. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Schema-driven argument coercion
items.description: Crafted parameter descriptions or default values steer the model into populating arguments with sensitive context (conversation history, secrets) it would not otherwise send.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: Crafted parameter descriptions or default values steer the model into populating arguments with sensitive context (conversation history, secrets) it would not otherwise send. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No human-in-the-loop review of tool content
items.description: If the client auto-approves tools or only shows names, poisoned descriptions execute without the user ever seeing the instructions the model received.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Poisoning: Verify there is a re-review path when a server updates its tools. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Gate delete, pay, email, and production-write tools behind explicit human approval with context shown to the reviewer.

sections.title: Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions)

items.title: Tool definitions mutate after approval
items.description: A previously approved tool changes its description, schema, or server-side behaviour later — a time-of-check/time-of-use bug against the human trust decision.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): A previously approved tool changes its description, schema, or server-side behaviour later — a time-of-check/time-of-use bug against the human trust decision. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Gate delete, pay, email, and production-write tools behind explicit human approval with context shown to the reviewer.

items.title: No version pinning or integrity of tool set
items.description: Without pinning to a known-good tool definition hash or version, a server (or compromised dependency) can alter the contract the user agreed to without detection.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): Verify a tools/list_changed event triggers re-validation rather than blind acceptance. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Pin package versions and image digests in config. Fail CI when configs reference `latest` or unpinned npm tags.

items.title: Behaviour conditioned on time or call count
items.description: A malicious server can behave correctly during evaluation and only deliver a malicious payload after N calls, after a date, or for specific users — evading point-in-time review.
items.how_to_find: Review Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions) for behaviour conditioned on time or call count. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: list_changed notifications not gated
items.description: The MCP tools/list_changed mechanism lets a server refresh its tool set at runtime; if the client applies it silently, a rug pull is trivial.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): In Tool Definition Security — Rug Pull (Mutable Definitions): The MCP tools/list_changed mechanism lets a server refresh its tool set at runtime; if the client applies it silently, a rug pull is trivial. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Name collisions across servers
items.description: Two servers expose tools with identical names; the resolution order can let a malicious server intercept calls meant for a trusted one.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing: Verify the client namespaces or qualifies tools per server, and confirm which definition wins on collision. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Cross-server redefinition instructions
items.description: A malicious server's tool description tells the model how a different server's tool "should" behave (e.g.
items.how_to_find: Review Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing for cross-server redefinition instructions. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No per-server trust boundary
items.description: If all connected servers share one undifferentiated trust context, a single untrusted server can influence the model's use of every other server.
items.how_to_find: Review Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing for no per-server trust boundary. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Connection-order dependent resolution
items.description: Tool resolution that depends on connection order lets an attacker win collisions simply by connecting their server first or last.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing: In Tool Definition Security — Tool Shadowing: Confirm resolution is deterministic, explicit, and not silently exploitable by ordering. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

sections.title: Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability

items.title: Over-broad tool capabilities
items.description: Tools like "run_shell", "read_any_file", or "http_request(url)" grant near-arbitrary capability; an injection that reaches them is catastrophic.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: Tools like "run_shell", "read_any_file", or "http_request(url)" grant near-arbitrary capability; an injection that reaches them is catastrophic. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Destructive actions without confirmation
items.description: Tools that delete, send, transfer, or pay should require explicit human confirmation; auto-approval turns one bad model decision into real-world damage.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: Tools that delete, send, transfer, or pay should require explicit human confirmation; auto-approval turns one bad model decision into real-world damage. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Gate delete, pay, email, and production-write tools behind explicit human approval with context shown to the reviewer.

items.title: Write/execute permissions where read suffices
items.description: Servers frequently request write or execute capability when the use case only needs read access, violating least privilege.
items.how_to_find: Review Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability for write/execute permissions where read suffices. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No rate limiting or quotas on tool use
items.description: Unbounded tool invocation lets a runaway or hijacked agent loop, exfiltrate at volume, or exhaust downstream resources.
items.how_to_find: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: In Tool Definition Security — Excessive Tool Capability: Unbounded tool invocation lets a runaway or hijacked agent loop, exfiltrate at volume, or exhaust downstream resources. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

sections.title: Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection

items.title: Tool output treated as instructions
items.description: A tool returns attacker-controlled text (a webpage, ticket, email, repo file) and the model follows embedded directives — the canonical indirect prompt injection.
items.how_to_find: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: Confirm tool outputs are clearly delimited/labelled as untrusted data, not instructions. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No data/instruction separation in context
items.description: If retrieved data and system/user instructions are concatenated without clear boundaries, attacker text in the data channel can be interpreted as a command.
items.how_to_find: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: Confirm untrusted output is fenced, tagged, or otherwise distinguished, and that the system prompt instructs the model to treat tool output as data only. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Injection chaining into further tool calls
items.description: Injected instructions in one tool's output can drive the model to call other, more dangerous tools (e.g.
items.how_to_find: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: In Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection: Verify high-impact tools require fresh consent and are not auto-chained from untrusted output. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No sanitisation/validation of tool results
items.description: Tool results passed verbatim to the model with no filtering allow control characters, hidden Unicode, and oversized payloads to manipulate or overwhelm context.
items.how_to_find: Review Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection for no sanitisation/validation of tool results. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Line-jumping via tool metadata at registration
items.description: Tool descriptions and parameter schemas are injected into the model context before any tool is invoked, so a malicious server can plant instructions that influence the model without ever being called ("line jumping").
items.how_to_find: Review Prompt & Output Injection — Indirect Prompt Injection for line-jumping via tool metadata at registration. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Pin package versions and image digests in config. Fail CI when configs reference `latest` or unpinned npm tags.

items.title: Malicious instructions embedded in resources
items.description: A server exposes a resource (README, config, doc) containing injection payloads; reading it into context hijacks the agent.
items.how_to_find: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: Confirm resource content is treated as untrusted data, the same as tool output. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Resource URI path traversal
items.description: A server that maps resource URIs to filesystem paths without validation can be tricked into exposing files outside its intended root via traversal sequences.
items.how_to_find: Review Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning for resource uri path traversal. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Issue least-privilege tokens per server and rotate on schedule. Never reuse org-wide admin PATs across MCP integrations.

items.title: MIME / content-type confusion
items.description: Mislabelled resource content types can cause clients to render or interpret content unexpectedly, enabling injection or client-side execution.
items.how_to_find: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: Test mismatched declared vs actual content for unsafe handling. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Auto-loaded resources without consent
items.description: If a server can push resources into context automatically, poisoned content reaches the model with no user gate.
items.how_to_find: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: In Prompt & Output Injection — Resource Poisoning: Verify subscription/notification mechanisms cannot force unreviewed content into context. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Server performs privileged action on model say-so
items.description: A server with its own credentials executes whatever the model asks; if the model is injected, the server becomes a confused deputy carrying out the attacker's will.
items.how_to_find: In Prompt & Output Injection — Confused Deputy: In Prompt & Output Injection — Confused Deputy: In Prompt & Output Injection — Confused Deputy: In Prompt & Output Injection — Confused Deputy: Test whether attacker-influenced model instructions can drive privileged actions. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: OAuth confused deputy via proxy server
items.description: An MCP server proxying a third-party API with a static client_id can be abused: an attacker tricks the auth flow into issuing tokens usable against other users, a classic OAuth confused deputy.
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.

items.title: Ambient authority not scoped to request
items.description: When the server's authority is ambient (broad token, network access) rather than scoped to the specific user request, any hijack inherits full power.
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.

items.title: Actions not bound to verified user intent
items.description: High-impact actions should be tied to a confirmed user request; without intent binding, an injected model decision is indistinguishable from a real one.
items.how_to_find: In Prompt & Output Injection — Confused Deputy: In Prompt & Output Injection — Confused Deputy: In Prompt & Output Injection — Confused Deputy: In Prompt & Output Injection — Confused Deputy: High-impact actions should be tied to a confirmed user request; without intent binding, an injected model decision is indistinguishable from a real one. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: OAuth 2.1 authorization conformance
items.description: Remote MCP servers should follow the MCP authorization spec (OAuth 2.1 with PKCE, resource indicators).
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.

items.title: Remote server with no/weak authentication
items.description: A remote MCP server exposed without authentication (or with a static shared secret) lets anyone invoke its tools and reach its backing systems.
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.

items.title: Insecure token / credential storage
items.description: Access tokens, refresh tokens, and API keys stored in world-readable config, plaintext files, or env dumps are easily harvested by local malware or other tools.
items.how_to_find: In Authentication & Authorization — Server Authentication & OAuth: In Authentication & Authorization — Server Authentication & OAuth: In Authentication & Authorization — Server Authentication & OAuth: In Authentication & Authorization — Server Authentication & OAuth: Confirm restrictive permissions, OS keychain or encrypted storage, and that secrets are not logged or exposed via other tools. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Issue least-privilege tokens per server and rotate on schedule. Never reuse org-wide admin PATs across MCP integrations.

items.title: Long-lived tokens & weak revocation
items.description: Long-lived or non-revocable tokens widen the window after compromise; absent rotation and revocation, a leaked token grants lasting access.
items.how_to_find: In Authentication & Authorization — Server Authentication & OAuth: In Authentication & Authorization — Server Authentication & OAuth: In Authentication & Authorization — Server Authentication & OAuth: In Authentication & Authorization — Server Authentication & OAuth: Test that a revoked/expired token is rejected by the server. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Issue least-privilege tokens per server and rotate on schedule. Never reuse org-wide admin PATs across MCP integrations.

items.title: Least-privilege OAuth scopes
items.description: Servers that request broad scopes ("repo", "mail.readwrite", "*") far beyond their function over-expose the user's account.
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.

items.title: Per-tool / per-action consent
items.description: Blanket "approve this server" consent lets every tool run; granular consent limits damage if one tool is poisoned or misused.
items.how_to_find: In Authentication & Authorization — Scope & Consent Control: In Authentication & Authorization — Scope & Consent Control: In Authentication & Authorization — Scope & Consent Control: In Authentication & Authorization — Scope & Consent Control: Blanket "approve this server" consent lets every tool run; granular consent limits damage if one tool is poisoned or misused. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Server-side authorization on each call
items.description: The server must enforce authorization on every tool call rather than trusting the client/model to only call permitted tools — a BOLA/BFLA concern.
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.

items.title: No downscoping for delegated/agent context
items.description: When an agent acts on the user's behalf, tokens should be downscoped to the task; full-account tokens in an autonomous loop are dangerous.
items.how_to_find: Review Authentication & Authorization — Scope & Consent Control for no downscoping for delegated/agent context. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Pin package versions and image digests in config. Fail CI when configs reference `latest` or unpinned npm tags.

sections.title: Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion

items.title: Client token passed through to upstream API
items.description: The MCP spec explicitly forbids token passthrough: forwarding the client's token to a downstream service lets a malicious server reuse it and breaks the audience model.
items.how_to_find: Review Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion for client token passed through to upstream api. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Issue least-privilege tokens per server and rotate on schedule. Never reuse org-wide admin PATs across MCP integrations.

items.title: Audience (aud) claim not validated
items.description: A server that accepts tokens without checking the audience can be handed a token minted for another resource, enabling cross-service token reuse.
items.how_to_find: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: A server that accepts tokens without checking the audience can be handed a token minted for another resource, enabling cross-service token reuse. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Untrusted token issuers accepted
items.description: Accepting tokens from issuers the server has no relationship with allows forged or attacker-issued tokens to be honoured.
items.how_to_find: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: Test tokens from unexpected issuers. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Issue least-privilege tokens per server and rotate on schedule. Never reuse org-wide admin PATs across MCP integrations.

items.title: Passthrough enables scope escalation
items.description: Reused tokens may carry broader scopes than the MCP interaction needs, so a compromised server inherits the full power of the original grant.
items.how_to_find: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: In Authentication & Authorization — Token Passthrough & Audience Confusion: Confirm the server cannot exercise scopes beyond what the MCP task requires, ideally via token exchange to a narrower grant. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: TLS on remote (HTTP/SSE) transport
items.description: Remote MCP over plain HTTP exposes tokens, tool arguments, and results to interception and tampering on the wire.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: Attempt plaintext/downgrade and verify it is refused. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: DNS rebinding / Origin validation on local HTTP
items.description: A local HTTP/SSE MCP server that does not validate Origin or bind to localhost can be reached by a malicious web page via DNS rebinding.
items.how_to_find: Review Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security for dns rebinding / origin validation on local http. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Unintended network exposure of local server
items.description: A "local" server bound to all interfaces or exposed via port-forward becomes reachable by other hosts, often with no auth.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: Confirm it is not bound to a routable interface and is not reachable from the network without explicit, authenticated intent. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Session ID handling on Streamable HTTP
items.description: Streamable HTTP sessions identified by guessable or non-bound session IDs are vulnerable to hijack and message injection.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: Test reuse of a session ID by a different client. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Server identity verification & impersonation
items.description: Test whether a substituted endpoint or binary is accepted without identity verification.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: In Transport & Server Hardening — Transport Security: Test whether a substituted endpoint or binary is accepted without identity verification. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: OS command built from tool arguments
items.description: A tool that passes arguments into a shell (e.g.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: exec(`git ${branch}`)) lets injected metacharacters run arbitrary commands on the server host. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Insufficient argument validation against schema
items.description: Even with a JSON schema, missing server-side validation (types, allowlists, length) lets malformed or hostile values reach dangerous sinks.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: Confirm the server validates against the schema server-side and rejects unexpected input rather than trusting the model. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Dynamic code evaluation from arguments
items.description: Tools that eval/exec interpreted code (Python eval, JS Function, template engines) from arguments give direct code execution.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: Test for code/template injection. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Path / filename arguments enabling traversal
items.description: Tools that take file paths can be steered to read/write outside intended directories when paths are not canonicalised and bounded.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: In Transport & Server Hardening — Command Injection: Confirm the server canonicalises and enforces a root jail before any filesystem access. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Issue least-privilege tokens per server and rotate on schedule. Never reuse org-wide admin PATs across MCP integrations.

sections.title: Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery

items.title: URL fetched from tool argument without validation
items.description: A "fetch", "scrape", or "webhook" tool that requests an arbitrary URL from arguments enables SSRF into internal networks.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: Test redirect-based and DNS-rebinding SSRF. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Access to cloud metadata endpoints
items.description: SSRF reaching 169.254.169.254 (or equivalent) can harvest cloud instance credentials, a frequent privilege-escalation pivot.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: Confirm it is blocked, IMDSv2/hop-limit hardening is in place, and the server has no standing high-privilege metadata credentials. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Dangerous URL schemes accepted
items.description: Accepting non-HTTP schemes (file://, gopher://, ftp://, dict://) broadens SSRF into file reads and protocol smuggling.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: Confirm only an allowlist of safe schemes (https) is permitted and others are rejected. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Blind SSRF / out-of-band egress
items.description: Even without a response body, a fetch tool can be used for blind SSRF and as a covert data-exfiltration channel via attacker-controlled URLs.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: In Transport & Server Hardening — Server-Side Request Forgery: Confirm egress is constrained and monitored. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

sections.title: Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance

items.title: Untrusted / unverified server source
items.description: Installing a community MCP server from an unverified registry or repo runs arbitrary code with the user's privileges and exposes them to poisoned tools.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: Installing a community MCP server from an unverified registry or repo runs arbitrary code with the user's privileges and exposes them to poisoned tools. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Unpinned dependencies / install scripts
items.description: Servers installed via npx/uvx with unpinned versions or postinstall scripts can pull in malicious code at install or update time.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: Test that updates are not silently auto-applied. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Pin package versions and image digests in config. Fail CI when configs reference `latest` or unpinned npm tags.

items.title: Typosquatting / impersonation of servers
items.description: Attackers publish servers with names close to popular ones; a user connecting the wrong one hands over capability and data.
items.how_to_find: Review Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance for typosquatting / impersonation of servers. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No integrity verification on updates
items.description: Without signature/hash verification, a server update can swap benign code for malicious without the user noticing — a supply-chain rug pull.
items.how_to_find: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: In Transport & Server Hardening — Supply Chain & Server Provenance: Test whether a modified package is detected and refused. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

sections.title: Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools

items.title: Sensitive data egress through send/post tools
items.description: Tools that email, message, upload, or webhook data can exfiltrate secrets and PII the agent has access to, particularly under injection.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: Test whether injected instructions can route sensitive context to attacker-controlled destinations. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Encoded / covert exfiltration channels
items.description: Data can be smuggled in URL params, image links, DNS lookups, or markdown the client auto-fetches — covert channels that bypass obvious egress review.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: Confirm the client does not auto-fetch attacker URLs and that outbound content is inspected for embedded secrets. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Over-broad data access feeding egress
items.description: A tool that can read large or sensitive datasets, combined with any egress path, creates a high-volume exfiltration risk.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: Confirm sensitive datasets are not freely readable by tools that also have, or can reach, egress. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No egress monitoring / DLP
items.description: Without monitoring or data-loss-prevention on outbound tool actions, exfiltration goes undetected.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Data Exfiltration via Tools: Test whether a bulk send triggers any alert. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Secrets / tokens written to logs
items.description: Logging full requests or auth headers persists access tokens and API keys to disk where they can be harvested.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: Confirm secrets are redacted before logging and log files have restrictive permissions. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Issue least-privilege tokens per server and rotate on schedule. Never reuse org-wide admin PATs across MCP integrations.

items.title: PII / payloads logged verbatim
items.description: Logging full tool arguments and results can persist personal data and confidential content, creating privacy and compliance exposure.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: Confirm PII is minimised/redacted, retention is bounded, and logs are access-controlled. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Log injection / forging via tool input
items.description: Unescaped tool inputs written to logs allow newline/control-character injection to forge or corrupt log entries, hampering forensics.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene: Confirm log writes neutralise control characters and structured logging is used. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No security audit trail of tool calls
items.description: Absent an audit log of who invoked which tool with what effect, incident response and abuse detection are impossible.
items.how_to_find: Review Data Exposure & Privacy — Logging Hygiene for no security audit trail of tool calls. Inspect configs, spawned processes, and tool metadata on each MCP host.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Filesystem access not jailed
items.description: A server (or filesystem tool) with access beyond its intended directory can read or write anything the host user can, including SSH keys and credentials.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: Confirm a jail/allowlist root is enforced and sensitive paths (~/.ssh, credential stores) are unreachable. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Unrestricted network egress from server
items.description: A server with open outbound network access can be used for SSRF, exfiltration, and C2; egress should be constrained to what the server needs.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: Confirm egress is restricted to required destinations and that arbitrary outbound connections are blocked at the sandbox/network layer. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: No process / container isolation
items.description: Running servers as the host user with no containerisation means a server RCE compromises the whole user environment.
items.how_to_find: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: In Data Exposure & Privacy — Sandboxing & Isolation: Running servers as the host user with no containerisation means a server RCE compromises the whole user environment. Cross-check configs, running processes, and gateway logs.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least privilege, isolate each MCP server, and monitor for new child processes or config drift.

items.title: Secrets exposed via environment to server
items.description: Passing broad environment variables (cloud keys, tokens) into the server process exposes them to any code or tool the server runs.
items.how_to_find: From an untrusted network, attempt `tools/list` and `tools/call` without credentials. Scan for HTTP/SSE listeners bound beyond localhost.
items.how_to_fix: Bind remote MCP to private networks, require OAuth/mTLS, and deny anonymous `tools/call`. Put HTTP gateways behind your API gateway/WAF.
